semiotic.positioning: Handcrafted luxury with deliberate restraint. Orange box as icon. No visible logos on most products. Typography and materials communicate exclusivity without declaration.
key_signals: no visible branding on leather goods

narrative.positioning: Equestrian heritage since 1837. Thierry Hermes, Parisian saddler. Six generations of family stewardship. The brand story is one of continuous craft refinement, not expansion.
key_signals: artisan training program (3+ years per craftsman)

ideological.positioning: Quiet excellence over conspicuous consumption. Anti-trend. Objects are made to last decades. Sustainability through durability rather than through messaging.

experiential.positioning: Tactile luxury. Every touchpoint is choreographed: the weight of the box, the ribbon, the tissue paper, the store architecture. Purchase is a ceremony.

social.positioning: Ultra-exclusive community. Waitlists as social filter. Ownership signals membership in a circle that cannot be bought into directly.

economic.positioning: Price is a structural barrier, not a value signal. Hermes does not compete on price. Price is set to preserve exclusivity and fund craftsmanship.

cultural.positioning: French haute couture tradition. Parisian craft culture. The brand is inseparable from French cultural identity and the luxury-as-art philosophy.

temporal.positioning: 187 years of continuous operation under the same family. One of the oldest luxury houses. Heritage is not marketed — it is the brand.
